items.note: Spring is the most beloved Japanese season — associated with 桜 (sakura = cherry blossoms) and renewal. The school and fiscal year start in April (spring), so 春 carries connotations of new beginnings and youth. 春一番 (haruichiban) = the first strong southerly wind of spring, which signals winter is ending. 春分 (shunbun) = spring equinox (national holiday). 春巻き (harumaki) = spring roll. 青春 (seishun) = youth/adolescence (literally 'blue spring'). 春眠 (shunmin) = spring drowsiness — a famous Chinese poem line: 春眠暁を覚えず (one doesn't notice the dawn in spring sleep).

Japanese summer is famous for its extreme heat and humidity (蒸し暑い, mushiatsui). Cultural touchstones: 花火大会 (hanabi taikai = fireworks displays), 盆踊り (bon odori = obon dancing), 縁日 (ennichi = festival stalls), かき氷 (kakigōri = shaved ice). 夏休み (natsuyasumi) = summer holiday. 夏至 (geshi) = summer solstice. 夏バテ (natsubate) = summer fatigue — a real condition from prolonged heat. 夏 as a kanji is complex: it contains components meaning a person in full display, like foliage at its peak.

Autumn is prized for its mild weather and 紅葉 (kōyō = autumn foliage). The leaves turn red (紅, kō) and yellow in mountain areas from October, reaching cities in November. 秋分 (shūbun) = autumn equinox (national holiday). 食欲の秋 (shokuyoku no aki) = 'autumn of appetite' — it's said food tastes best in autumn. 読書の秋 (dokusho no aki) = 'autumn of reading'. 秋 contains 禾 (grain) + 火 (fire) — grain drying over fire, the harvest season.

Japan's winter varies dramatically by region: Hokkaido gets world-class powder snow (パウダースノー) while Tokyo often has no snow at all. 冬至 (tōji) = winter solstice — people eat 柚子 (yuzu) in a bath (柚子湯, yuzuyu) and 南瓜 (kabocha = pumpkin) to ward off winter illness. 冬休み (fuyuyasumi) = winter holiday. 雪見 (yukimi) = snow viewing (like hanami but for snow). 冬眠 (tōmin) = hibernation (winter + sleep). 防寒 (bōkan) = cold protection. The kanji 冬 shows icicles hanging — two 冫 strokes at the bottom.

Japan's rainy season, running roughly June to mid-July (later in Hokkaido, which barely has one). 梅 = plum tree, 雨 = rain — named because plums ripen in this wet period. Despite being written 梅雨 (baiu in on'yomi), it's almost always read 'tsuyu' in everyday speech. The constant humidity (湿度, shitsudo, can exceed 90%) makes this the least popular season for tourists. Counter-tip: 紫陽花 (ajisai = hydrangeas) are beautiful in June — check temple gardens. 梅雨明け (tsuyuake) = end of rainy season.

晴 contains 日 (sun) + 青 (blue/clear) — literally 'the sun making the sky blue'. 晴れ is the noun/adjective, 晴れる (hareru) is the verb (to clear up). 快晴 (kaisei) = perfectly clear sky, no clouds at all (快 = pleasant). 晴れ着 (haregi) = fine clothes worn on special occasions — the metaphor of 'clear day' meaning celebratory. 晴れの日 (hare no hi) means both a clear day and an important/celebratory day. Weather forecasts: 晴れ ☀ / 曇り ☁ / 雨 🌧 are the standard icons.

曇 contains 日 (sun) + 雲 (cloud) — the sun behind a cloud. 雲 (kumo) alone = cloud. 曇る (kumoru) = to become cloudy. 薄曇り (usugumori) = lightly overcast. 曇りのち雨 (kumori nochi ame) = 'cloudy then rain' — standard weather forecast format. のち (nochi) = after/later, a very useful word for forecasts. 雲 and 曇 share the 雲 component: look for the cloud radical in weather kanji.

大 (ō/dai) = big. 大雨警報 (ōame keihō) = heavy rain warning — take these seriously in Japan, as heavy rain can trigger landslides (土砂崩れ, doshakuzure). 土砂降り (doshaburi) = heavy downpour (土砂 = soil/sand cascading). 小雨 (kosame) = light rain / drizzle (小 = small). 霧雨 (kirisame) = misty rain / drizzle (霧 = mist). 雨 appears often: 梅雨 (tsuyu), 雨季 (uki = rainy season in tropics), 雨水 (usui = second solar term, 'rain water').

雷 literally means 'thunder-god-sound': 雨 (rain) + 田 (rice field) — the rain that strikes the paddy fields. 雷 is the thunder and the lightning together. 雷雨 (raiu) = thunderstorm. 稲妻 (inazuma) = lightning specifically (稲 = rice plant, 妻 = wife — 'the wife/consort of the rice plant', because lightning was thought to fertilise the rice). The god of thunder is 雷神 (Raijin), depicted with drums. During storms: 雷が鳴る (kaminari ga naru) = thunder rumbles.

台 = platform/stand, 風 = wind — a wind that 'stands up' and dominates. Japan is hit by 20–30 typhoons a year from the Pacific, peaking in August–September. 強風 (kyōfū) = strong wind. 暴風 (bōfū) = violent storm. When a 台風 warning (台風警報) is issued, trains stop, shops close — this is not an exaggeration. 台風の目 (taifū no me) = eye of the typhoon — 目 = eye, also used idiomatically for the calm centre of any crisis. 風 alone reads 'kaze': 風邪 (kaze) = cold (illness) — literally 'wind illness'.

霧 contains 雨 (rain) + 務 (duty/task) — rain that is doing its job of obscuring. 霧 is heavier fog; 靄 (moya) is light mist. 霧雨 (kirisame) = drizzle. 霧島 (Kirishima) = famous volcanic area in Kyushu (literally 'fog island'). 霧笛 (kiriteki) = foghorn. 朝霧 (asagiri) = morning mist. 霧 is one reason Japanese mountain photography is so atmospheric — 雲海 (unkai = sea of clouds) is a popular photo subject from mountaintops.

天気 (tenki) = weather. 予報 (yohō) = forecast (予 = beforehand/in advance, 報 = report). 予報士 (yohōshi) = weather forecaster. Japan's weather forecasts are considered among the most accurate in the world. The Japan Meteorological Agency (気象庁, kishōchō) issues hourly updates. Useful forecast vocabulary: 降水確率 (kōsui kakuritsu) = precipitation probability, 最高気温 (saikō kion) = maximum temperature, 最低気温 (saitei kion) = minimum temperature.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION: 暑い (atsui) = hot weather or room temperature. 熱い (also atsui!) = hot to the touch or dangerously hot (hot coffee, hot bath, fever). Same pronunciation, different kanji, very different meaning. 暑い contains 日 (sun) — hot like the sun. 熱い contains 火 (fire) at the bottom — hot like fire, burning hot. 暑中見舞い (shochū mimai) = summer greeting card sent during the hottest period. 猛暑 (mōsho) = intense/extreme heat. 残暑 (zanshō) = lingering summer heat (after 立秋, the official start of autumn).

Pair this with item 13 (暑い). 熱い is for things that burn: お茶が熱い (the tea is hot), お風呂が熱い (the bath is too hot). 熱 (netsu) alone = heat / fever: 熱がある (netsu ga aru) = I have a fever. 情熱 (jōnetsu) = passion (emotion-heat). 熱心 (nesshin) = enthusiastic (hot-heart). 熱帯 (nettai) = tropical (heat zone). The key test: could it burn you if you touched it? → 熱い. Is it just warm/hot in the air? → 暑い.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION: 寒い (samui) = cold weather or ambient cold. 冷たい (tsumetai) = cold to the touch (cold water, ice cream, a cold hand). 寒い is how you feel walking outside in winter. 冷たい is how you feel touching ice. 寒気 (kanki / samuke) = chill/cold air, or a shiver (two readings!). 極寒 (gokkan) = extreme cold. 寒波 (kanpa) = cold wave. In comedy, a bad joke is called 寒い (samui) — 'cold', like the feeling of a joke falling flat. 寒中水泳 (kanchū suiei) = midwinter swimming, a New Year tradition.

Pair with 寒い (item 15). 冷たい = physically cold to touch: 冷たい水 (cold water), 冷たいアイス (cold ice cream), 手が冷たい (hands are cold). 冷 (rei/tsumetai) also appears in: 冷蔵庫 (reizōko = refrigerator), 冷凍 (reitō = frozen), 冷静 (reisei = calm/composed — 'cold and clear-headed'). Figuratively, 冷たい can describe a cold/unfriendly person: あの人は冷たい = 'that person is cold/distant'. 冷やす (hiyasu) = to cool something down.

暖かい = pleasantly warm. Contains 日 (sun) — warmed by the sun. 暖房 (danbō) = heating (room heating). 暖流 (danryū) = warm ocean current. 温かい (also atatakai!) = warm — used for food, drink, or warm feelings (温かい人 = warm-hearted person). 暖 tends to be weather/air; 温 tends to be liquids or emotions. 温泉 (onsen) = hot spring. 体温 (taion) = body temperature. 温度 (ondo) = temperature (general). 気温 (kion) = air/atmospheric temperature.

涼 = refreshing coolness — the pleasant cool of autumn or a breeze. Contains 氵 (water radical) + 京 (capital) — water from the mountains flowing to the capital. This is NOT cold; it's the perfect temperature. 涼む (suzumu) = to cool oneself, enjoy the cool air. 夕涼み (yusuzumi) = enjoying the cool of a summer evening — a classic Edo-period summer activity. 涼しい顔 (suzushii kao) = a cool/unruffled expression (looking unfazed under pressure). Often used: 秋は涼しくて気持ちいい = 'Autumn is cool and pleasant'.

蒸し (mushi) = steaming. 蒸す (musu) = to steam (as in cooking). 蒸し暑い = 'steamy hot' — the oppressive humid heat of Japanese summer. Japan has two problem seasons: freezing winter and sweltering muggy summer. 湿気 (shikke/shitki) = humidity/dampness. 湿度 (shitsudo) = humidity percentage. 梅雨 (tsuyu) and August are the peak 蒸し暑い periods. Tip: 扇子 (sensu) = folding fan, 団扇 (uchiwa) = flat fan — both essential summer items.

気 (ki) = air / atmosphere / spirit / feeling. 温 (on) = warmth / temperature. 気温 specifically means the temperature of the air — what a thermometer in the shade reads. 最高気温 (saikō kion) = maximum temperature. 最低気温 (saitei kion) = minimum temperature. 平均気温 (heikin kion) = average temperature. 体温 (taion) = body temperature. 水温 (suion) = water temperature. 室温 (shitssuon) = room temperature. Japan uses Celsius exclusively — a good temperature reference: 0°C (freezing), 25°C (comfortable), 35°C (hot warning), 38°C+ (危険, kiken = dangerous).

度 (do) = degree (temperature, angle, time). 今日は三十度です = 'Today is 30 degrees'. 度 also counts: 一度 (ichido) = once / one degree, 二度 (nido) = twice / two degrees. 態度 (taido) = attitude (literally 'degree of manner'). 温度計 (ondokei) = thermometer (温度 + 計 = counter/meter). 緯度 (ido) = latitude, 経度 (keido) = longitude — both use 度. 今何度ですか (ima nando desu ka) = 'What temperature is it now?' is a useful travel phrase.

降る (furu) = to fall (precipitation). 雨が降る = rain falls. 雪が降る (yuki ga furu) = it snows. 降る is ONLY used for things falling from the sky (rain, snow, ash, even meteor showers: 流星が降る). Don't confuse with 振る (also furu) = to wave/shake, or 古い (furui) = old. 降水量 (kōsuiryō) = precipitation amount. 雨が降りそうです (ame ga furisō desu) = 'it looks like it will rain'. 土砂降りです (doshaburi desu) = 'it's pouring down'.

Same verb 降る as 雨が降る. 雪 (yuki) = snow — one of the most evocative words in Japanese poetry. 初雪 (hatsuyuki) = first snowfall of the year. 大雪 (ōyuki) = heavy snowfall. 雪だるま (yukidaruma) = snowman (daruma = the round Daruma doll). 雪かき (yukikaki) = snow shovelling. 雪見 (yukimi) = snow viewing, a cultural pleasure. Hokkaido's powder snow (パウダースノー) is world-famous for skiing. 雪 in names: 雪子 (Yukiko, a common female name). 雪解け (yukidoke) = snowmelt — also means a thaw in relations.
